{{< include latex-macros/macros.qmd >}}

$$
\ub{\hess(\vb)}_{(p+1) \times (p+1)} = \sumin \ub{\olive{\hess_i(\vb)}}_{(p+1) \times (p+1)}
$${#eq-logit-hessian}

$$
\ba
\olive{\hess_i(\vb)} &\eqdef \deriv{\vb\'}\scorei
&& \text{(definition of the Hessian component)}
\\
&= \deriv{\vb\'}\paren{\vx_i\eps_i}
&& \text{(score component for one observation)}
\\
&= \vx_i \deriv{\vb\'}\eps_i
&& \text{(constant-factor rule: } \vx_i \text{ does not depend on } \vb \text{)}
\\
&= \vx_i \violet{\eps_i'}
&& \text{(definition: } \violet{\eps_i'} \eqdef \deriv{\vb\'}\eps_i \text{)}
\ea
$${#eq-logit-hessian-i}

{{< slidebreak >}}

{{< include _subfiles/logistic-regression/_thm-d_pi_d_beta.qmd >}}

{{< slidebreak >}}

Using @thm-d_pi_d_beta:

$$
\ba
\violet{\eps'_i} &\eqdef \derivf{\eps_i}{\vb\'}
&& \text{(definition of } \violet{\eps'_i} \text{)}
\\
&= \deriv{\vb\'}(y_i - \mu_i)
&& \text{(definition of the error: } \eps_i \eqdef y_i - \mu_i \text{)}
\\
&= \deriv{\vb\'}y_i - \deriv{\vb\'}\mu_i
&& \text{(linearity of differentiation)}
\\
&= \mathbf{0}_{1 \times (p+1)} - \deriv{\vb\'}\mu_i
&& \text{(} y_i \text{ does not depend on } \vb \text{)}
\\
&= -\derivf{\mu_i}{\vb\'}
&& \text{(additive identity)}
\\
&= -\purple{\derivf{\pi_i}{\vb\'}}
&& \text{(Bernoulli conditional mean: } \mu_i = \pi_i \text{)}
\\ &= - \pi_i (1-\pi_i) \vx_i\'
&& \text{(transpose of the gradient of } \pi \text{ w.r.t. coefficients)}
\\ &= - \Varf{Y_i|\vX_i=\vx_i} \vx_i\'
&& \text{(Bernoulli conditional variance: } \Varf{Y_i|\vX_i=\vx_i} = \pi_i(1-\pi_i) \text{)}
\ea
$$

{{< slidebreak >}}

Returning to @eq-logit-hessian-i:

$$
\ba
\olive{\hess_i(\vb)} &= \vx_i \violet{\eps_i'}
&& \text{(Hessian component identity)}
\\ &= \vx_i \paren{- \Varf{Y_i|\vX_i=\vx_i} \vx_i\'}
&& \text{(substitute } \violet{\eps_i'} \text{)}
\\ &= \olive{-\ub{\vx_i}_{(p+1) \times 1} \ub{\Varf{Y_i|\vX_i=\vx_i}}_{1 \times 1} \ub{\vx_i\'}_{1 \times (p+1)}}
&& \text{(move the scalar factor } -\Varf{Y_i|\vX_i=\vx_i} \text{ between the vectors)}
\ea
$${#eq-logit-hessian-i-2}

{{< slidebreak >}}

Returning to @eq-logit-hessian:

$$
\ba
{\llik''(\vb)} &= \sumin \olive{\hess_i(\vb)}
&& \text{(the Hessian decomposes over observations)}
\\ &= {-\sumin \ub{\vx_i}_{(p+1) \times 1}  \ub{\Varf{Y_i|\vX_i=\vx_i}}_{1 \times 1} \ub{\vx_i\'}_{1 \times (p+1)}}
&& \text{(Hessian component for one observation)}
\\ &= {- \ub{\mX\'}_{(p+1) \times n} \ub{\matr{D}}_{n \times n} \ub{\mX}_{n \times (p+1)}}
&& \text{(matrix form: } \matr{D} \eqdef \text{diag}(\Varf{Y_i|\vX_i=\vx_i}) \text{)}
\ea
$${#eq-logistic-hess}

where $\matr{D} \eqdef \text{diag}(\Varf{Y_i|\vX_i=\vx_i})$
is the diagonal matrix whose $i^{th}$ diagonal element is $\Varf{Y_i|\vX_i=\vx_i}$.

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#rem-hessian-nsd}

#### The logistic log-likelihood is concave

The Hessian $\llik''(\vb) = -\mX\'\matr{D}\mX$ (@eq-logistic-hess)
is negative semi-definite for every $\vb$,
because the diagonal entries of $\matr{D}$ are
$\Varf{Y_i|\vX_i=\vx_i} = \pi_i(1-\pi_i) \geq 0$.
Since the diagonal entries are non-negative,
$\matr{D}^{1/2} \eqdef \text{diag}\paren{\sqrt{\pi_i(1-\pi_i)}}$ is well-defined,
and for any vector $\vec{v} \in \reals^{p+1}$:

$$
\ba
\ub{\tp{\vec{v}}}_{1 \times (p+1)} \paren{-\ub{\mX\'}_{(p+1) \times n}\ub{\matr{D}}_{n \times n}\ub{\mX}_{n \times (p+1)}} \ub{\vec{v}}_{(p+1) \times 1}
&= -\tp{\vec{v}}\mX\'\matr{D}\mX\vec{v}
&& \text{(the scalar } -1 \text{ factors out)}
\\ &= -\tp{\vec{v}}\mX\'\matr{D}^{1/2}\matr{D}^{1/2}\mX\vec{v}
&& \text{(} \matr{D} = \matr{D}^{1/2}\matr{D}^{1/2} \text{)}
\\ &= -\tp{\paren{\matr{D}^{1/2}\mX\vec{v}}}\paren{\matr{D}^{1/2}\mX\vec{v}}
&& \text{(transpose of a product; } \matr{D}^{1/2} \text{ is diagonal, hence symmetric)}
\\ &= -\normsq{\matr{D}^{1/2}\mX\vec{v}}
&& \text{(definition of the squared Euclidean norm)}
\\ &\leq 0
&& \text{(squared norms are non-negative)}
\ea
$$

A twice-differentiable function
whose Hessian is negative semi-definite everywhere is concave,
so the log-likelihood $\llik(\vb)$ is concave,
and every stationary point
(every solution of the score equation $\vec{\llik'}(\vb) = \mathbf{0}_{(p+1) \times 1}$)
is a global maximum.
In fact, since $\pi_i = \expitf{\vx_i \cdot \vb} \in (0,1)$ strictly,
every diagonal entry $\pi_i(1-\pi_i)$ is strictly positive;
so when $\mX$ has full column rank,
$\matr{D}^{1/2}\mX\vec{v} = \mathbf{0}_{n \times 1}$ only for $\vec{v} = \mathbf{0}_{(p+1) \times 1}$,
the Hessian is negative definite,
the log-likelihood is strictly concave,
and the maximum is unique.
Consequently, when the [Newton-Raphson algorithm](intro-MLEs.qmd#sec-newton-raphson) converges,
it converges to the unique global maximum likelihood estimate,
not merely to a local optimum.

:::

Compare with [the linear regression Hessian](Linear-models-overview.qmd#eq-lm-hess):

$$
\ba
\llik''(\vb) &= -\frac{1}{\ss}\sumin \ub{\vx_i}_{(p+1) \times 1} \ub{\vx_i'}_{1 \times (p+1)}
&& \text{(linear-model Hessian)}
\\ &= -\ub{\mX\'}_{(p+1) \times n} \ub{\matr{D}^{-1}}_{n \times n} \ub{\mX}_{n \times (p+1)}
&& \text{(matrix form: here } \matr{D} = \ss \idmat_{n \times n} \text{, so } \matr{D}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\ss}\idmat_{n \times n} \text{)}
\ea
$${#eq-linear-hess}

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exr-hess-logistic}

Determine the elements of the Hessian matrix for logistic regression.

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#sol-hess-logistic}

The components of the Hessian are:

$$
\ba
\ub{\hess(\beta)}_{(p+1) \times (p+1)}
&= \xderiv{\beta\'}{\beta}{\llik}
&& \text{(definition of the Hessian)}
\\
&= \deriv{\beta\'}\ub{\score}_{(p+1) \times 1}
&& \text{(the Hessian is the derivative of the score)}
\\
&= \sbmat{
\ub{\derivf{\score}{\beta_0}}_{(p+1) \times 1}
& \ub{\derivf{\score}{\beta_1}}_{(p+1) \times 1}
& \cdots
& \ub{\derivf{\score}{\beta_p}}_{(p+1) \times 1}
}
&& \text{(differentiate column-by-column w.r.t. } \beta\' \text{)}
\\
&=
\ub{\sbmat{
\dderivf{\llik}{\beta_0}
& \xderivf{\llik}{\beta_0}{\beta_1}
& \cdots
& \xderivf{\llik}{\beta_0}{\beta_p}
\\
\xderivf{\llik}{\beta_1}{\beta_0}
& \dderivf{\llik}{\beta_1}
& \cdots
& \xderivf{\llik}{\beta_1}{\beta_p}
\\
\vdots
& \ddots
& \ddots
& \vdots
\\
\xderivf{\llik}{\beta_p}{\beta_0}
& \xderivf{\llik}{\beta_p}{\beta_1}
& \cdots
& \dderivf{\llik}{\beta_p}
}}_{(p+1) \times (p+1)}
&& \text{(each score entry is itself a partial derivative of } \llik \text{)}
\ea
$$

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#exr-hess-beetles}

Determine the Hessian for the `beetles` model.

:::

{{< slidebreak >}}

:::{#sol-hess-beetles}

In the `beetles` model, the Hessian is:

$$
\ba
\ub{\hess(\beta)}_{2 \times 2} &=
\sbmat{
\ub{\derivf{\score}{\beta_0}}_{2 \times 1}
& \ub{\derivf{\score}{\beta_1}}_{2 \times 1}
}
&& \text{(differentiate column-by-column; here } p+1 = 2 \text{)}
\\
&=
\sbmat{
\dderivf{\llik}{\beta_0}
& \xderivf{\llik}{\beta_0}{\beta_1}
\\
\xderivf{\llik}{\beta_1}{\beta_0}
& \dderivf{\llik}{\beta_1}
}
&& \text{(each score entry is a partial derivative of } \llik \text{)}
\\
&=
-\sumin
\ub{\pmat{1 \\ c_i}}_{2 \times 1}
\ub{\pi_i(1-\pi_i)}_{1 \times 1}
\ub{\pmat{1 & c_i}}_{1 \times 2}
&& \text{(logistic Hessian with } \vx_i = (1, c_i)\' \text{)}
\\
&=
\sbmat{
-\sumin\pi_i(1-\pi_i)
& -\sumin c_i \pi_i(1-\pi_i)
\\
-\sumin c_i \pi_i(1-\pi_i)
& -\sumin c_i^2 \pi_i(1-\pi_i)
}
&& \text{(multiply out the outer products, entry by entry)}
\ea
$$

:::
